hints: Compare only the first n coins of each: P(Emilia more)=P(Ron more)=a, P(tie)=b.
If already ahead/behind on n coins, the extra coin can't change it; only a tie is broken by it (heads, prob 1/2).
solution: On the first $n$ coins each, let $a=P(\text{Emilia ahead})=P(\text{Ron ahead})$ and $b=P(\text{tie})$, with $2a+b=1$. Emilia's last coin only matters on a tie, which she wins w.p. $\tfrac12$. So $P=a+\tfrac b2=a+\tfrac{1-2a}{2}=\tfrac12.$

hints: Let X~U(0,½) (left) and Y~U(½,1) (right); want Y−X>1/3.
Writing p=X, q=Y−½ (both U(0,½)), the event is p−q<1/6.
solution: Scale $L=1$ with $X\sim U(0,\tfrac12)$ and $Y\sim U(\tfrac12,1)$. Setting $p=X,\,q=Y-\tfrac12$ (both $U(0,\tfrac12)$), $Y-X>\tfrac13\Leftrightarrow p-q<\tfrac16$. The complementary region $p\ge q+\tfrac16$ has area $\tfrac1{18}$ out of $\tfrac14$, i.e. probability $\tfrac29$. So $P=1-\tfrac29=\tfrac79.$

hints: Only the +30% branch finishes in the money: S=10.4, payoff 2.4.
The flat and −50% branches pay 0.
solution: The call pays only on the $+30\%$ move ($S=10.4$, payoff $2.4$); the flat and $-50\%$ outcomes pay $0$. So the price is $0.2\times2.4=0.48.$

hints: Condition on the original pattern: all distinct, one pair, or all same.
Each pattern's reproduction probability: 6/216, 3/216, 1/216 respectively.
solution: By pattern: all-distinct originals (prob $\tfrac{120}{216}$) re-match w.p. $\tfrac6{216}$; one-pair (prob $\tfrac{90}{216}$) w.p. $\tfrac3{216}$; all-same (prob $\tfrac6{216}$) w.p. $\tfrac1{216}$. Total $=\dfrac{120\cdot6+90\cdot3+6\cdot1}{216^2}=\dfrac{996}{46656}=\dfrac{83}{3888}.$

hints: β(Y on X)=S_xy/S_xx; β(X on Y)=S_xy/S_yy.
Here S_xx=2 < S_yy=50 (y-values are more spread out).
Dividing by a larger denominator decreases β ⇒ −1.
solution: $\beta_{Y|X}=\tfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$ while $\beta_{X|Y}=\tfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{yy}}$. Since the $y$'s are more spread ($S_{yy}=50>S_{xx}=2$), the slope shrinks, so $\beta$ decreases: answer $-1.$

hints: x=50cosθ ⇒ dx/dt = −50 sinθ·dθ/dt.
dθ/dt = −4/(50·0.8) = −1/10 rad/min (decreasing at 1/10).
solution: From $x=50\cos\theta$, $\tfrac{dx}{dt}=-50\sin\theta\,\tfrac{d\theta}{dt}$. At $x=30$, $y=40$, so $\sin\theta=0.8$ and $\tfrac{d\theta}{dt}=-\dfrac{4}{50(0.8)}=-\dfrac1{10}$ rad/min — decreasing at $\tfrac1{10}.$

hints: Each prime may appear in at most one element; pick elements to maximize the total.
Use big primes 11,13,17,19,23,29 directly; for {2,3,5,7} prefer 28=2²·7, 27, 25 over the bare primes.
solution: Pairwise-coprimality means each prime is used by at most one element. Take the large primes $11,13,17,19,23,29$ as-is, and for the small primes use $28=2^2\cdot7$, $27=3^3$, $25=5^2$ (better than the bare primes), plus $1$. The sum is $1+28+27+25+11+13+17+19+23+29=193.$

hints: Track states (7-vessel, 11-vessel); each fill/empty/pour is one transaction.
Repeatedly fill the 7 and pour into the 11, emptying the 11 when full.
The minimum is 14 transactions.
Payout = (number of 1–3 rolls) if you end on a 4/5, else 0.
Stops are 4/5 (cash, prob 2/3 of stops) or 6 (bust); E[continues]=1 (geometric, p=1/2).
solution: Each roll continues (1–3) w.p. $\tfrac12$, so the number of continues before stopping has mean $1$. A stop is a cash-out (4/5) w.p. $\tfrac23$ and a bust (6) w.p. $\tfrac13$, independent of the count. The payout is the count if cashed, else $0$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac23\cdot1=\tfrac23.$

hints: An ATM call has delta ≈ 0.5.
Use the Taylor expansion ΔC ≈ δ·ΔS + ½Γ·(ΔS)².
Add the change to the initial price 2.3.
Unfold the box into a flat plane to straighten the path along the surface.
Compare √((a+b)²+c²) over the three ways of pairing the sides.
Divide the shortest distance by the speed 2.5 and round.
Put-call parity: C − P = S − Ke^{−rt}, which is linear in S.
The linear term has zero second derivative, so Γ_call = Γ_put.
solution: By put-call parity $C-P=S-Ke^{-rt}$, whose second derivative in $S$ is zero. Hence the call and put share the same gamma: $\Gamma_{\text{put}}=0.02.$
